title: How Low Can We Go? Exploring Minimal Assumptions in Quantum Cryptography
speaker: Dakshita Khurana
abstract:
In this talk, I will explore the fascinating landscape of assumptions in quantum cryptography—especially, how little we need to assume to build secure quantum protocols. We will cover key cryptographic primitives including quantum encryption, signatures, and money, and show how these primitives imply the existence of one-way puzzles, a quantum analogue of classical one-way functions. I will also highlight the utility of one-way puzzles and discuss concrete assumptions that enable their realization, revealing intriguing connections to quantum advantage.
No prior quantum background will be assumed. This talk is based on joint works with Kabir Tomer.

proof_stages.label: Minimal-assumption question for quantum cryptography
proof_stages.summary: The talk contrasts classical cryptography’s reliance on one-way functions with unconditional quantum key distribution, then asks what complexity assumptions are necessary and sufficient for quantum cryptographic primitives beyond QKD.

proof_stages.label: Distilling public-secret hardness into one-way puzzles
proof_stages.summary: Public-key encryption and signatures are used to isolate a minimal public-data/secret-key recovery hardness, formalized first as one-way state generators and then as one-way puzzles sampled by a quantum algorithm with classical outputs.

proof_stages.label: Classicalizing one-way states by shadow tomography
proof_stages.summary: Efficient shadow tomography converts many copies of a one-way state into a classical shadow string whose overlap-estimation property lets inversion hardness transfer from the quantum state to the classical puzzle output.

proof_stages.label: Lower bound from #P-oracle inversion of puzzles
proof_stages.summary: Because a P^#P machine can invert efficiently quantum-sampled one-way puzzles, any primitive that implies such puzzles requires the separation P^#P != BQP, while commitments and secure computation remain notable exceptions.

proof_stages.label: Commitments and secure computation as positive targets
proof_stages.summary: Oracle evidence for quantum pseudorandom states motivates weaker assumptions, and one-way puzzles are positioned as a bridge yielding commitments and, via quantum information techniques, secure computation.

proof_stages.label: Quantum-advantage probability hardness as a source
proof_stages.summary: #P-complete permanent computation and sampling-based quantum-advantage experiments suggest hard output-probability approximation problems, but directly sampling a hard value together with its public instance fails because the value itself is not efficiently obtainable.

proof_stages.label: Distributional puzzle via next-bit sampling
proof_stages.summary: A distributional version of one-way puzzles is built by truncating a quantum-sampled output and asking for the next bit; any successful reverse sampler would approximate output probabilities, so average-case probability-approximation hardness from quantum-advantage conjectures yields one-way puzzles and hence commitments and secure computation.

proof_stages.label: Assumption comparison and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The talk summarizes that the construction draws hardness from the larger quantum/#P region rather than classical NP-style assumptions, notes limitations of the puzzle-based route, and poses open problems on worst-case hardness, stronger quantum primitives, and physical hardness sources.

examples.name: Bennett--Brassard quantum key distribution
examples.note: QKD is used as the established unconditional quantum-cryptographic primitive that motivates asking what can be done beyond key distribution.
examples.engagement_reason: Bennett--Brassard QKD is cited as an assumption-free 1984 achievement, without explaining or running the protocol.
